video_title: Will the Fed Buy Stocks? | Animal Spirits 473
gist: Ben Carlson and Michael Batnick weigh the bull vs. bear case for the AI-driven market, concluding that cheapening valuations (especially Nvidia's forward P/E) and stable analyst EPS accuracy make the bull case stronger than the bear case for now. They lay out a bigger structural thesis that US households now hold more wealth in stocks than real estate, making equities systemically important enough that the Fed could someday step in and buy stocks directly during a severe downturn, and review data suggesting AI has so far been net job-creating rather than a labor-market killer.

insights.claim: IBM fell 23% pre-market after disappointing earnings despite being roughly a $270 billion company and one of the 50 largest in the world, showing that even mega-caps can still suffer sudden single-day 'flash crash'-style drops.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Size alone no longer protects a stock from violent single-day repricing on an earnings miss; a reminder that idiosyncratic risk persists even in mega-caps.

insights.claim: A Bank of America chart shows hyperscaler free cash flow crashing at almost the same rate semiconductor company free cash flow is rising, an inverse 'trade-off' the hosts called the chart of the year, and they argue this transfer of cash from hyperscalers to chipmakers cannot continue indefinitely.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Watch hyperscaler free cash flow trends as a leading indicator of AI capex fatigue; a reversal would pressure semiconductor earnings that are currently absorbing that cash.
insights.quote: This chart cannot continue. It just can't.

insights.claim: Meta released a low-priced agentic coding model (Muse Spark 11) through its own model API, raising the question of whether hyperscalers will eventually undercut each other on AI model pricing in a way that erodes their own profitability even as capex stays high.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: A price war among AI model providers would be bearish for hyperscaler margins even if AI usage keeps growing, decoupling revenue growth from profitability.

insights.claim: The average absolute daily price change for the semiconductor index has been 3.36% over the last 50 days, a volatility level historically only seen during the COVID crash, the 2008 financial crisis, and the dot-com bubble unwind.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Elevated realized volatility in AI-linked chip stocks signals crowded, leveraged positioning that can amplify moves in either direction.
insights.quote: 3.36% has been the average absolute daily change over the last 50 days, and you only see spikes like that during the COVID crash, during the financial crisis, and during the dot bubble unwind.

insights.claim: Goldman Sachs' momentum factor had one of its largest three-week sell-offs on record, down roughly 8% over three weeks, which the hosts read as a healthy unwind of crowded, leveraged momentum positioning rather than a broad market signal.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: A momentum-factor unwind of this size can look like a market-wide selloff but is more about crowded positioning than deteriorating fundamentals.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers have barely begun borrowing to fund AI capex and still have substantial unused borrowing capacity, meaning the current capex cycle could extend well beyond what current spending levels suggest if they choose to lever up further.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Watch hyperscaler debt issuance as a signal of how much longer the AI capex supercycle can run; low current leverage means the cycle isn't obviously near its ceiling.
insights.quote: They haven't really even started borrowing that much money yet. They're just dipping their toe in the water. They still have so much capacity to borrow if they want to keep putting their foot on the gas pedal.

themes.theme: AI bubble debate versus valuation reality

insights.claim: One host compiled 10 reasons to be bearish (circular hyperscaler capex, Mag Seven underperformance, AI spending bleeding into GDP, retail investors 'all in,' inflation still around 3.5%, mortgage rates near 7%, complacency after three straight years of double-digit gains, AI 'checking every bubble box,' an overdue recession after 17 years, and stretched multi-year annualized returns) but concluded the bear case is weaker than the bull case because the bullish reasons are happening now while the bearish reasons are things that could happen but currently aren't.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: A structured bull/bear checklist for the AI trade; investors should track which of the 10 bearish conditions (especially a recession) actually starts materializing rather than assuming the bubble thesis by default.
insights.quote: The bearish arguments are way flimsier because the bullish reasons are happening now. The bearish reasons are things that could happen, but they're not happening.

insights.claim: Nvidia's valuation on a forward P/E basis is the cheapest it has been since early 2019, according to a Bloomberg chart, because earnings have grown faster than the stock price even after Nvidia's massive multi-year rally.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Falling forward P/E despite a huge run-up undercuts the simple 'Nvidia is a bubble stock' narrative and suggests the market may be pricing in more durable earnings than headline price gains imply.
insights.quote: Bloomberg had a chart that shows Nvidia's valuation is the cheapest since early 2019 on a PE ratio, which is kind of nuts to think about.

insights.claim: Forward P/E ratios for technology stocks are currently below both their 10-year and 5-year averages and near the lowest levels since the start of 2025, meaning tech valuations are improving even as many commentators call this a technology bubble.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Cheapening forward valuations weaken the 'obviously overvalued' bubble argument and suggest the AI trade may be more earnings-driven than multiple-expansion-driven.
insights.quote: Tech stock valuations are improving during what many think is a technology bubble.

insights.claim: Analysts' forward S&P 500 EPS estimates have historically been accurate 67% of the time, with actual earnings landing within 5% of estimates, and estimates only diverge meaningfully from actuals during recessions.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: A genuinely bearish call on current high forward-earnings expectations is effectively a recession call, not a valuation call, since estimates are historically reliable outside of downturns.
insights.quote: They are quite accurate 67% of the time. Actual earnings are within 5% of estimates.

insights.claim: Retail investors' net single-stock buying has fallen to a new post-COVID low, per Vanda Research data, which complicates the 'retail is all-in and driving the market' bear argument, though part of the decline may reflect retail shifting into single-stock ETFs instead.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: If retail single-stock buying is genuinely fading rather than just migrating to ETF wrappers, one commonly cited late-cycle warning sign is weaker than assumed.
insights.quote: Retail investors' net single stock buying has fallen to a new post-COVID low.

insights.claim: The S&P 500 ex-Technology Index (ticker SPXT) closed at a new all-time high even while a meaningful share of technology names sit in a bear market, indicating the rest of the market is quietly strong.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Broadening strength outside of tech reduces concentration risk narrative and suggests the rally isn't solely a mega-cap tech phenomenon.
insights.quote: The S&P 500 ex-Technology Index closed the week at a new all-time high.

insights.claim: Advance-decline lines for the S&P 500, S&P 400, and S&P 600 have all pushed to fresh all-time highs, and the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) is also at all-time highs, signaling broad market participation despite tech-sector turbulence.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Broad advance-decline strength is a healthier technical signal than a narrow mega-cap-led rally and argues against an imminent top driven by internal weakness.
insights.quote: The advanced decline lines for the S&P 500, 400, and 600 have all pushed to fresh all-time highs.

insights.claim: Small-cap strength is itself partly an AI trade: about 24% of the Russell 2000 index is tied to AI in some form (tech is only ~18% of the index, industrials ~17% and also AI-exposed), and 52% of the Russell 2000's total first-half return came from AI-tied companies, according to Sonu of the Carson Group.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Small-cap outperformance shouldn't be read as a pure 'AI trade rotation into value' story; much of the Russell 2000's gains are still effectively an AI-exposure bet, mainly via industrials.
insights.quote: 24% of the Russell 2000 index is tied to AI in some form. 52% of the total first half return comes from companies tied to AI.

themes.theme: Household wealth shift and the 'Fed buy stocks' thesis

insights.claim: US household exposure to equities is at a record high, and the stock market is now a significantly greater component of total household net worth than real estate, a reversal that has never occurred before at this scale.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: If equities are now the dominant store of American household wealth, a severe bear market has larger macro (consumption, wealth-effect) consequences than in prior real-estate-dominated eras.
insights.quote: The stock market is a significantly greater component of total household net worth than real estate now, which blows my mind.

insights.claim: The shift toward equities dominating household net worth is structural, not cyclical, driven by the post-1980s rise of 401(k) and IRA-based stock ownership: only about 4% of Americans owned stocks in the 1950s, rising to about 19% by 1983, versus roughly 55-60% of Americans owning stocks today.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Investors betting on a reversion to pre-1980s low equity ownership as a mean-reversion trade are likely fighting a permanent structural shift, not a temporary cyclical extreme.
insights.quote: In the 1950s it was like 4% of people own stocks. By 1983 it was 19%.

insights.claim: Because roughly 55-60% of Americans now own stocks (per Eric Balchunas' Bloomberg analysis), the stock market has become systemically important enough that the Fed could plausibly buy stocks directly during a severe future downturn, similar to what the Bank of Japan and China's central bank have already done.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: A tail scenario worth pricing: direct central-bank equity purchases during the next crisis would put a much firmer floor under US stocks than markets currently assume.
insights.quote: I think he's absolutely right. I think it could happen. It would not shock me at all.

insights.claim: Because the stock market is now systemically important to household wealth, future bear markets are more likely to resemble fast 'flash crashes' that force policymakers into quick reversals than slow grinding declines, citing 2008's TARP reversal, COVID-era stimulus, and the 2025 tariff 'Liberation Day' 90-day pause as precedents.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Investors should expect sharper, shorter drawdowns followed by rapid policy reversals rather than prolonged bear markets, changing the calculus for buying dips versus waiting out declines.
insights.quote: Bare markets in the future are going to be flash crashes.

insights.claim: Michael Batnick argued that sending direct stimulus checks is the most effective way to fight a recession, and defended the policy despite the 2021 inflation surge by arguing the inflation was driven primarily by supply-chain disruption and a demand/supply imbalance rather than the checks themselves.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: If the next recession occurs without a comparable supply-chain shock, direct stimulus checks could be deployed again with less inflation risk than the 2021 experience implies.
insights.quote: Part of the reason why we got the inflation was not just the checks, it was that the supply chain was turned off, and there was a huge imbalance of way too much demand and not enough of supply.

insights.claim: US software development job postings are up 15% since Claude Code's launch in late February 2025, even as overall job postings fell 7% over the same period, and 71% of the gains came from senior roles rather than junior hiring, per Indeed's chief economist.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Direct evidence against the thesis that AI coding tools are shrinking software engineering headcount; hiring for the roles AI is supposedly best at replacing is actually rising.
insights.quote: US software development job postings are up 15% since the launch of Claude Code in late February 2025, while overall job postings fell by 7% over the same period.

insights.claim: The unemployment rate for workers aged 20-24 has been essentially unchanged since ChatGPT's launch in late 2022, undercutting the widely repeated fear that AI is disproportionately displacing young, junior white-collar workers.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: The most feared cohort for AI displacement (entry-level white-collar workers) shows no unusual deterioration in employment data nearly three years into widespread generative AI adoption.

insights.claim: The recent pattern of professional and business-services job growth rising and falling looks more like a pandemic-era hiring 'bullwhip' normalization (overhiring followed by correction) than an AI-driven layoff wave.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Attributing white-collar job softness to AI may be premature; pandemic-era hiring distortions offer a simpler, more consistent explanation for the recent data.

insights.claim: Despite continually more capable AI models, rising token consumption, and growing familiarity with AI tools, white-collar employment trends look better now than they did a year ago, meaning the repeated 'wait six months' AI-doomer predictions about mass white-collar job losses have not come true so far.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: The AI-driven-layoffs bear case for markets and the economy has repeatedly failed to show up in the data; investors should demand actual evidence rather than assume the disruption is imminent, while noting adoption may still be early.
insights.quote: As of right now, they are [wrong]. Now, everyone keeps saying just wait. These models are going to get better. People are going to use them more. Companies haven't even started.

themes.theme: Fixed income's improved margin of safety

insights.claim: The taxable-equivalent yield on high-yield municipal bonds is around 9%, roughly matching typical private-credit yields of about 9%, giving investors a liquid alternative that offers comparable income to illiquid private credit.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Investors chasing private-credit yield for income may be able to get comparable after-tax income in a liquid muni-bond wrapper instead, avoiding illiquidity risk.
insights.quote: Look at the taxable equivalent yield on high yield munis. 9%. Compared to private credit, also around 9%.

insights.claim: Current fixed-income yields (roughly 5% to 9% depending on credit quality) give investors a far bigger margin of safety than at any point in the last 15 years, reversing the 'rewardless risk' dynamic of the 2010s through early 2020s that pushed capital into private credit in search of yield.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Bonds are once again a genuinely attractive, lower-risk income option, reducing the need to reach into illiquid or riskier credit for yield.
insights.quote: You have a way bigger margin of safety in bonds than you've had for any time in the last 15 years.

insights.claim: In June, higher-income households' after-tax wage growth eased while lower-income cohorts' wage growth improved to a similar level, per Mike Zaccardi's data, suggesting the 'K-shaped economy' divergence narrative was partly a cyclical phase rather than a permanent structural break.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Investment and consumer-spending theses built on a permanent, widening income divergence should be reassessed; the underlying data show the gap is narrowing, not widening.

insights.claim: Investors who started in Bitcoin ETFs at their January 2024 launch lost an average of 5.8% annually on a dollar-weighted basis even though Bitcoin itself roughly rose from about $46,000 to around $60,000+, because investors chased performance by buying and selling at the wrong times, per analysis referencing Jason Zweig and Jeffrey Ptak.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Even a rising underlying asset can produce negative investor returns due to behavioral timing mistakes; a cautionary data point for anyone chasing recent Bitcoin ETF performance.
insights.quote: In aggregate, people who started out in the Bitcoin funds lost an average of 5.8% annually jumping in and out of these funds.

insights.claim: Rising GLP-1 drug adoption (11% of US adults per this year's Gallup survey, likely undercounted) coincides with sweet-snack sales down 17% and broader US snack sales down 4% over the past four years, including at Smucker's Hostess snack division.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: GLP-1 adoption is showing up as a measurable demand headwind for packaged-snack and confectionery companies, a trend worth tracking for consumer-staples exposure.
insights.quote: In this year's Gallup survey, 11% of US adults currently take GLP-1.

insights.claim: Comcast is splitting its cable and broadband businesses because each has become 'an anchor on the other,' and spun-off sister company Versant's Fandango/movie-ticketing platform segment is its only growing business line, generating $826 million in 2025 (up 3.9%) and accelerating to 9.5% revenue growth in Q1 2026.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Legacy media conglomerates continue to break apart declining linear/cable assets from growing digital-adjacent businesses; Fandango's accelerating growth stands out as a bright spot within an otherwise shrinking media portfolio.
insights.quote: Fandango... did $826 million in 2025, up 3.9%. The only Versant segment that grew year-over-year, and it's accelerating. Q1 2026 platforms revenue rose 9.5% driven by Fandango movie ticketing.

insights.claim: Netflix's all-at-once release model causes shows to fall out of the cultural conversation within days as viewers binge and move on, while HBO's weekly-episode release model sustains audience engagement over weeks, helping explain why Netflix season-two shows often crash in viewership (e.g., White Lotus grew its audience 63% from season 1 to 2, House of the Dragon dropped only 8% between seasons) while comparable Netflix shows do not sustain audiences across seasons.
insights.speaker: co-host, Animal Spirits
insights.so_what: Release-cadence strategy (weekly vs. binge) is a meaningful lever for subscriber retention and content ROI, a factor worth weighing for streaming-platform investment theses.
insights.quote: Netflix gives you the whole season at once and most people finish it in four to six days. After that, the show falls out of the conversation.
